flashcards.back: A bus is a shared communication pathway that connects multiple components of a computer system, allowing data transfer between CPU, memory, and I/O devices.

flashcards.front: What are the three main types of buses in a computer system?
flashcards.back: The three main types are: DATA BUS (carries actual data), ADDRESS BUS (carries memory addresses), and CONTROL BUS (carries control signals like read/write, interrupt).
Bus arbitration is the process of determining which device gains control of the bus when multiple devices want to use it simultaneously, preventing conflicts.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a point-to-point bus and a shared bus?
flashcards.back: Point-to-point bus connects two devices directly with a dedicated path. Shared bus connects multiple devices to a common pathway, requiring arbitration.
Bus width refers to the number of bits the bus can transfer simultaneously. A 32-bit bus can transfer 32 bits of data in one clock cycle.
Bus clock rate is the frequency at which the bus operates, measured in MHz or GHz. It determines how fast data can be transferred per second.
Bus bandwidth is the maximum data transfer rate, calculated as: Bandwidth = (Bus Width × Clock Rate) / 8 bytes per second.
In daisy-chain arbitration, devices are connected in series. Each device has a grant line passed to the next, giving priority based on physical position.
Centralized arbitration uses a single bus controller (arbiter) to grant bus access to devices based on priority schemes like fixed priority or rotating priority.
Distributed arbitration lets devices negotiate for bus control among themselves without a central controller, using protocols like collision detection.
Bus multiplexing combines multiple signals onto the same physical lines to save pins and space, using time-division or frequency-division multiplexing.
A bus protocol defines the rules for communication on the bus, including timing, signal levels, command sequences, and error handling procedures.
Wait states are additional clock cycles added when a slow device cannot keep up with the bus speed, allowing slower memory or I/O devices to respond.
Bus bridging connects two different bus systems, allowing devices from one bus standard to communicate with another through a bridge chip.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous buses?
flashcards.back: Synchronous buses use a common clock and fixed timing. Asynchronous buses use handshaking signals between devices, adapting to different device speeds.
